{{Quote|Excuse me if I don't shake hands. The field only causes mild electrical burns.|Atton Rand, to the Jedi Exile, as he stands in a force cage|<ref name="kotor2" />}}
A '''force cage''' was a device that was designed for and used to contain [[Force-sensitive/Legends|Force-sensitive]] prisoners, the first examples of which were seen some time prior to [[3956 BBY]] during the [[Jedi Civil War]]. Existing through to the era of the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic]], these types of detention cells were, in essence, energy [[Deflector shield/Legends|shields]] that took the form of a tube. The device itself consisted of a base and cap that was connected by a column at the back that also anchored it to a wall. This assemblage served as a conduit for the energy barrier, keeping it running and anchoring it in place. Force cages were found throughout [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]], on [[Planet/Legends|worlds]] ranging from the urbanized [[City/Legends|city]]-planet of [[Taris/Legends|Taris]] to the remote and desolate [[Sith/Legends|Sith]] world of [[Korriban/Legends|Korriban]], and even aboard [[Warship/Legends|warships]] such as the [[Leviathan (Interdictor-class cruiser)|''Leviathan'']], the [[Flagship/Legends|flagship]] of the [[Darth Revan's Sith Empire|Sith Empire's]] war [[Fleet/Legends|fleet]]. During the years prior to the [[Galactic Civil War/Legends|Galactic Civil War]], a portable version of the still-popular device was developed; these types of cages were often employed by [[Bounty hunter/Legends|bounty hunters]], such as [[War'qi]] and [[Jango Fett/Legends|Jango]] and [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]], who wished to ensure the continued confinement of a live capture until delivery.

==Description==
{{Quote|You will find them ''[the Exile's companions]'' in the main irrigation channel room in the northern part of the plateau interior. The particle emitters there that once governed the flow of water to Telos can double as force cages.|One of the [[Handmaiden Sisters]], to the Jedi Exile|<ref name="kotor2" />}}
[[File:Malforia_electrocuted.jpg|thumb|left|250px|A force cage electrocutes its prisoner.]]
Most force cages were deceptively simple devices that resembled octagonal tubes when activated. The frame itself, permanently mounted to a wall or bulkhead, stood approximately two [[Meter/Legends|meters]] in height, with the base slightly below floor level in most instances.<ref name="kotor" /> The [[Force field/Legends|force fields]] that these types of detention cells emitted varied in color from bright [[blue]] to [[orange]], and could carry a charge that ranged from a small shock to incapacitation, including the inflicting of minor electrical burns.<ref name="kotor2" /> These apparatuses could also be modified to project a sort of "[[Torture/Legends|torture]] field" that, when activated, sent waves of extremely painful electrical currents into and through those trapped inside; this effect too could be modulated. Such fields could be ramped up in intensity to lethal proportions, serving as an ''ad hoc'' execution device.<ref name="kotor" /> Typical force cages included plenty of room for a detainee to either stand or sit; some force cages, such as the ones in the [[Telosian Jedi Academy]] that had been converted from water regulation pipes, were large enough for a grown [[Human/Legends|Human]] to sleep in.<ref name="kotor2" />

Later-model force cages, particularly those in use during the [[Imperial Era/Legends|era]] of the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire]], were made of a light yet durable ceramic material; portable models were typically two meters long, two high, and four wide once erected. Still portable even after assembly, the frame also provided an additional electrical deterrent against attempted breakout. Assembly was usually fairly simple, requiring only basic knowledge of security technology in order to set them up; however, they were not without flaws. Typical models of portable force cages were considerably weaker than their more permanent counterparts; incorrect assembly would negate the shocking effect of the device's force field, and even if properly set up, a being of sufficient strength could still batter their way through either the field or the frame.<ref name="gg10" />

==History==
{{Quote|Well this is a step up from a force cage, at least.|Atton Rand, after he, Kreia and the Exile are placed under house arrest on [[Citadel Station]]|<ref name="kotor2" />}}
[[File:CrewEbHwkTortured.jpg|thumb|left|230px|[[Revan/Legends|Revan]], [[Bastila Shan]], and [[Carth Onasi]] being tortured in force cages aboard the [[Leviathan (Interdictor-class cruiser)|''Leviathan'']]]]

Although most force cages used nonlethal barriers, they could and often were tweaked to cause minor amounts of pain when uncooperative prisoners attempted to bash their way through the energy barrier.<ref name="kotor2" /> Force cages could also be modified to double as torture devices by installing feedback nodes that would project powerful electric shocks into the victim contained within. Examples of such modifications could be found within the [[Davik Kang's estate|estate]] of [[Taris/Legends|Tarisian]] [[Crime lord/Legends|crime lord]] [[Davik Kang]], who used such a device to torture the former [[Pilot/Legends|pilot]] of his [[Starship/Legends|ship]], the ''[[Ebon Hawk]]''. Other cages that had been modified thusly were located aboard the [[Leviathan (Interdictor-class cruiser)|''Leviathan'']], [[Admiral/Legends|Admiral]] [[Saul Karath]]'s [[Sith/Legends|Sith]] [[Flagship/Legends|flagship]], in which he tortured the captive [[Revan/Legends|Revan]], [[Carth Onasi]], and [[Bastila Shan]] as the former [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Republic]] officer interrogated them about their mission to find the [[Star Forge]]. These cells could also be used to perform executions via electrocution or poison gas, such as the ones in the [[Tarisian military base|Sith military base]] located on Taris, or the [[Republic Embassy (Manaan)|Republic Embassy]] on the neutral world of [[Manaan/Legends|Manaan]]. Cages of this type were also found within the [[Sith Academy (Korriban)|Sith Academy]] on [[Korriban/Legends|Korriban]].<ref name="kotor" />

When they were first developed some time prior to [[3956 BBY]], force cages were often found in groups contained within a separate, often locked room within police stations, security rooms, and other such places throughout [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]]. One such place was within offices of the [[Telos Security Force]] aboard the massive [[Citadel Station]] in [[Orbit/Legends|orbit]] over [[Telos IV]], where the [[Meetra Surik|Jedi Exile]] and her companions, [[Kreia]] and [[Atton Rand]], were briefly held in the wake of the destruction of the [[Peragus Mining Facility]]. These banks of detention cells were typically controlled through a central [[Computer/Legends|computer]] interface terminal, allowing barriers to be raised or lowered in any number of combinations at once; when an [[Batu Rem impostor|assassin]], working for the [[Exchange]] criminal syndicate and posing as TSF officer [[Batu Rem]], attempted to kill the Exile and her party, he used a similar console to short out the security [[Holocamera/Legends|holocams]] as well to shut down the cages in which they were held.<ref name="kotor2" />

[[File:DarNala-NewGalacticOrder.jpg|thumb|right|230px|Dar'Nala experiences a force cage's torture field.]]
The [[Sith Empire (Post–Great Hyperspace War)|reformed Sith Empire]] made use of force cages during the [[Great Galactic War]] and its aftermath, during which the Sith Imperials installed the devices on [[Harrower-class dreadnought|Sith warships]] to transport prisoners of war. One such prisoner was the [[Togruta/Legends|Togruta]] [[Jedi Master/Legends|Jedi Master]] [[Dar'Nala]], who was captured by the forces of Imperial [[Captain/Legends|Captain]] [[Sivill]] in [[3653 BBY]], in the immediate aftermath of the signing of the [[Treaty of Coruscant]]. Sivill, under the impression that Dar'Nala had violated the terms of the treaty, used the cage's torture field while interrogating the Jedi Master on her perceived misconduct. Dar'Nala was adamant in her claims of innocence, and so incurred heavy punishment via electric shock. She was able to withstand most of the torture, but apparently succumbed to the jolts after Sivill ordered her execution. In truth, Dar'Nala survived even these shocks, albeit grievously scarred as a result of the high voltage.<ref name="ToP" />

[[File:MalgusPrisonBreak.png|thumb|left|250px|Darth Malgus in a force cage prison on [[Carrick Station]]]]
During the era of the Galactic Empire, a portable version, with a frame manufactured from ceramic compounds, was developed that had been designed to be collapsible for use in the field.<ref name="gundark" /> Once assembled, these types of portable force cages, similar in form and function to those that could be found within Imperial detention centers located throughout [[Imperial Space]], were still somewhat mobile once they were erected and activated.<ref name="gg10" /> These devices were often used by [[Bounty hunter/Legends|bounty hunters]], in instances when they needed to secure the live capture of a target, either for rendition to whoever had posted the original [[Bounty/Legends|bounty]], or for other purposes.<ref name="gundark" /> One popular model, the [[Model S-3|S-3]], which was manufactured by [[Damorind Securities]],<ref name="gg10" /> had been purchased by a bounty hunter named [[War'qi]] and installed in the cargo hold of his ship.<ref name="gundark" /> One of the six holding cells within the [[Firespray-31-class patrol and attack craft/Legends|Firespray-31-class patrol and attack craft]] [[Slave I/Legends|''Slave I'']], the vessel of [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] and his father [[Jango Fett/Legends|Jango Fett]]<ref name="SLA1" /> before him, was a force cage.<ref name="SWS" /> On the planet [[Questal/Legends|Questal]], the [[Merchant/Legends|merchant]] [[Ellam|Honest Ellam]]—owner of [[Honest Ellam's Speeder Lot]]—utilized a force cage to keep three [[Caniphant/Legends|caniphants]] restrained. He would let the predatory creatures out at night to patrol the Speeder Lot to ward off intruders.<ref name="GC">''[[The Game Chambers of Questal]]''</ref>

==Behind the scenes==
Force cages were first presented in passing in the [[1987]] [[West End Games]] [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game|roleplaying]] publication ''[[The Star Wars Sourcebook]]'' as an [[in-universe]] account of the [[Prison/Legends|prison]]-like cargo hold found in bounty hunter Boba Fett's [[Starship/Legends|starship]], ''Slave I''. Subsequent West End Games sourcebooks would reprint this information, without additional elaboration concerning the type of force cage to be found in the vessel.<ref name="Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back">''[[Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back]]''</ref><ref name="The Movie Trilogy Sourcebook">''[[The Movie Trilogy Sourcebook]]''</ref><ref name="The Star Wars Sourcebook Second Edition">[[The Star Wars Sourcebook (Second Edition)|''The Star Wars Sourcebook'', Second Edition]]</ref><ref name="Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back Second Edition">[[Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back (Second Edition)|''Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back'', Second Edition]]</ref><ref name="Star Wars Trilogy Sourcebook, Special Edition">''[[Star Wars Trilogy Sourcebook, Special Edition]]''</ref> Nonetheless, ''[[Galaxy Guide 10: Bounty Hunters]]'', did expand upon the general characterization, and a force cage was described as a portable detention device similar to fixed installations that could be found within Imperial prisons.

The publication of ''[[The Essential Guide to Vehicles and Vessels]]'' introduced a conflict with the established [[Star Wars Legends|continuity]] by declaring that the cargo hold aboard ''Slave I'' was occupied by prisoner cages, in addition to a force cage that was specifically intended to contain a [[Force-sensitive/Legends|Force-sensitive]] individual. Similar information was repeated in ''[[Star Wars: Behind the Magic]]''. This did not match the previous description of a force cage. However, an earlier series of comics, ''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire]]'', had depicted a type of containment device for Force-wielding individuals called a [[Universal Energy Cage]]. The continuity was further complicated by [[The Official Star Wars Fact File 26|''The Official Star Wars Fact File'' 26]], which stated that ''Slave I'' contained a universal energy cage and prison cages, but did not mention force cages. ''[[The New Essential Guide to Vehicles and Vessels]]'' noted that ''Slave I'' included a Force-wielding prisoner cell. Other publications, such as the ''[[Star Wars: Incredible Cross-Sections]]'' and the ''[[Star Wars: Complete Cross-Sections]]'', did not specify the type of prisoner containment found within ''Slave I''. In the [[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire (comic series)|''Shadows of the Empire'']] [[Star Wars comic books|comic book]], there were three story panels that depicted prisoner containment systems aboard ''Slave I''; however, they were not identified, and their types were unclear.

In contrast to previous depictions, the models of force cages that appear in the ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic]]'', ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic II: The Sith Lords]]'', and ''[[The Old Republic, Threat of Peace Act 2: New Galactic Order]]'' are shown to be permanent or semi-permanent constructions that are immobile once built and do not have a collapsible counterpart. Other, similar devices are featured in ''[[Star Wars]]'' lore, such as the [[Containment field/Legends|containment field]] from [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars'': Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']], and the [[mobile stockade]] from ''[[Showdown at Centerpoint]]''.
